223	
224	\begin{proof}
225	  \uses{def:Jacobian, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
226	  The Lean formalisation projects the field $\mathtt{geomIrred} : \mathrm{GeometricallyIrreducible}\, J.\mathtt{hom}$ of the witness.
227	
228	  Mathematically, the Albanese variety $\Jac(C)$ is the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$ (the Picard scheme), and the identity component of a group scheme of finite type over a field is geometrically irreducible (in fact, geometrically connected, and since it is smooth at the identity by Theorem~\ref{thm:Jacobian_smooth_genus} it is geometrically irreducible). Equivalently, in the symmetric-power route, $\Jac(C)$ arises as the Stein factorisation of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ followed by a translation; $\Sym^g(C)$ is geometrically irreducible (being the quotient of $C^g$, itself geometrically irreducible as a product of geometrically irreducible factors, by a finite group action), so its image under the Stein factorisation is geometrically irreducible as well.
229	\end{proof}
230	
231	\subsection{Sanity check in low genus}
232	
233	\begin{itemize}
234	  \item $g = 0$: Then $\Jac(C) = \Spec k$, the trivial group scheme; $\Jac(\mathbb P^1_k) = \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $0 = g$.
235	  \item $g = 1$: Then $\Jac(C) \cong C$ once a $k$-rational point on $C$ is fixed; $C$ is itself an elliptic curve (abelian variety of dimension $1$).
236	  \item $g \ge 2$: $\Jac(C)$ is a non-trivial $g$-dimensional abelian variety, never isomorphic to $C$ as a scheme.
237	\end{itemize}
238	
239	\section{Existence of an Albanese variety}
240	
241	The classical construction of the Jacobian goes through the Picard scheme machinery: $\Jac(C)$ is the connected component of the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. An equivalent route, available whenever a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ has been fixed, is to take the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ and translate by $-gP$.
242	
243	\subsection{The Albanese witness bundle}
244	
245	Before stating the existence theorem, we package its conclusion into a single structure --- the \emph{Albanese witness} of $C$. This bundling reverses the quantifier order of the classical statement (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}) and is the data structure consumed by the \texttt{Jacobian} family of declarations in this file and by the \texttt{AbelJacobi} interface of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi}.
246	
247	\begin{definition}
248	
249	\leanok
250	[Albanese witness for a curve]
251	  \label{def:JacobianWitness}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.JacobianWitness}
253	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus}
254	  An \emph{Albanese witness} for the smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ is a bundle of the following seven fields:
255	  \begin{itemize}
256	    \item $J$: an object of the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$ --- the candidate Albanese object.
257	    \item $\mathtt{grpObj}$: a group-object structure on $J$ in the category of $k$-schemes over $\Spec k$.
258	    \item $\mathtt{proper}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is proper.
259	    \item $\mathtt{smooth}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth.
260	    \item $\mathtt{geomIrred}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is geometrically irreducible.
261	    \item $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$: a proof that the structure morphism $J \to \Spec k$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$. This refines $\mathtt{smooth}$ at the specific relative dimension $\genus\, C$ (see Remark~\ref{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}).
262	    \item $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$: a proof that, for every $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ together with its group-object, proper, smooth, and geometrically-irreducible structures satisfies the Albanese universal property for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}.
263	  \end{itemize}
264	  Formally, an Albanese witness is a Lean-level record (a structure) whose name is \texttt{JacobianWitness}.
265	\end{definition}
266	
267	\begin{remark}
268	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_quantifier_order}
269	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness}
270	  The classical phrasing of the existence statement of an Albanese variety is
271	  \[
272	    \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \exists\, J,\ J \text{ is an Albanese object for } (C, P).
273	  \]
274	  The witness bundle reverses these quantifiers: a single $J$ is chosen once (intrinsic to $C$), and the marked-point-dependent data is recorded as the family-valued field
275	  \[
276	    \mathtt{isAlbaneseFor} \colon \forall\, P \in C(k),\ \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J.
277	  \]
278	  Mathematically this reversal is justified because the underlying scheme of any Albanese object is intrinsic to $C$: a different choice of $P \in C(k)$ alters the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism of $J$, and so leaves $J$ unchanged as a scheme. The reversed bundling is what makes the per-$P$ projection in Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} clean: $\mathtt{Jacobian.ofCurve}$, $\mathtt{Jacobian.comp\_ofCurve}$, and $\mathtt{Jacobian.exists\_unique\_ofCurve\_comp}$ each take a marked point $P$ as input and project the corresponding component of $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}\,P$ via the extraction trio (Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese_ofCurve}, Lemmas~\ref{lem:IsAlbanese_comp_ofCurve}, \ref{lem:IsAlbanese_exists_unique_ofCurve_comp}).
279	\end{remark}
280	
281	\begin{remark}
282	  \label{rem:JacobianWitness_smooth_redundancy}
283	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness}
284	  The fields $\mathtt{smooth}$ and $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ of an Albanese witness are mathematically redundant: the relative-dimension version implies the unqualified version via Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothOfRelativeDimension.smooth}. Keeping both fields is a Lean-level convenience: $\mathtt{smooth}$ is the form required by the typeclass binder of \texttt{IsAlbanese} (and by the protected $\mathtt{Jacobian.instGrpObj}$ projection), whereas $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ is the form required by the protected $\mathtt{Jacobian.smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}$ projection. The redundancy carries no mathematical cost (the two fields agree) and no soundness cost (deriving one from the other would only re-run the same Mathlib instance synthesis at the projection sites).
285	\end{remark}
286	
287	\begin{theorem}\leanok
288	[Existence of an Albanese variety uniformly over the marked point]
289	  \label{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
290	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.nonempty_jacobianWitness}
291	  % Cycle-fix (iter-149 plan-agent): drop the spurious \uses{def:Jacobian}
292	  % from this statement-block. Mathematically `def:Jacobian` is the entity
293	  % that depends on this existence theorem (def:Jacobian's \uses{...} lists
294	  % thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness), not the other way around. Listing
295	  % def:Jacobian here as a hypothesis of the existence theorem introduced
296	  % a 2-cycle in plastex's depgraph (def:Jacobian ↔ thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness)
297	  % that crashes `leanblueprint web` with RecursionError in depgraph.ancestors.
298	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, def:JacobianWitness}
299	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Exercise ex:jac (generalized Jacobians of a family of curves form a smooth quasi-projective family of relative dimension $p_a$, projective iff $X/S$ smooth); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
300	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume $X/S$ is projective and flat, its geometric fibers
301	  % are integral curves of arithmetic genus $p_a$, and $S$ is Noetherian.
302	  % Show the ``generalized Jacobians'' $\IPicz_{X_s/k_s}$ form a smooth
303	  % quasi-projective family of relative dimension $p_a$.  And show this
304	  % family is projective if and only if $X/S$ is smooth."
305	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Exercise (ex:jac).}
306	  Let $C$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over a field $k$. There exists a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $J$ over $k$ of relative dimension $g(C)$ such that, for every $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$, the scheme $J$ is an Albanese object for the pointed curve $(C, P)$ in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:IsAlbanese}. Equivalently, the type of Albanese witnesses for $C$ (Definition~\ref{def:JacobianWitness}) is non-empty.
307	\end{theorem}
308	
309	\begin{proof}
310	  \uses{def:IsAlbanese, def:genus, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:genusZeroWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar}
311	  The proof is split into three classical constructions plus the genus-$0$ sub-case absorbed into the same witness (the latter packaged as the explicit construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}, which routes through \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} --- rigidity proved over the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$ --- and then \emph{descends} the resulting morphism equality from $\bar k$ to the base field $k$ along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$). Each route produces the same intrinsic scheme $J$; only the universal morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to J$ varies with the marked point $P$. We outline each route and name the Mathlib infrastructure each one requires.
312	
313	  \paragraph{(0) Reduction to a $P$-independent witness.}
314	  The witness $J$ depends only on $C$, not on the marked point $P$. Indeed, both classical constructions below produce $J$ as an intrinsic object of $C$ (the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$, or the Stein-factorisation target of $\Sym^g(C) \to \Pic^g_{C/k}$ translated by a fixed $P_0$); a different choice of $P$ in $C(k)$ alters $\iota_P$ by a translation of $J$, which is an automorphism, and so does not change $J$ as a scheme. The Lean encoding reflects this by bundling $J$ as a single field and the universal-property data as $\forall P,\, \mathrm{IsAlbanese}\, C\, P\, J$. We now turn to producing $J$.
315	
316	  \paragraph{Route A --- Picard scheme.}
317	  This route follows Hartshorne~III.4 and FGA Explained, Chapter~9.
318	
319	  \begin{itemize}
320	    \item[\textbf{(A.1)}] \emph{The relative Picard functor.} For a $k$-scheme $T$, write $C_T := C \times_k T$ and let $\pi \colon C_T \to T$ be the projection. The relative Picard functor is
321	    \[
322	      \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k} \colon (\Sch/k)^{\mathrm{op}} \to \Sets, \qquad
323	      T \longmapsto \Pic(C_T) / \pi^*\Pic(T).
324	    \]
325	    A $T$-point is a line bundle on $C_T$ modulo a line bundle pulled back from $T$. The set-valued formulation suffices for representability; the canonical abelian-group structure on $\Pic(C_T)$ descends to the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ and gives $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ a natural group-functor refinement.
326	
327	    \item[\textbf{(A.2)}] \emph{Representability.} For $C$ smooth proper geometrically connected over $k$ with a $k$-rational point, $\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}$ (after \'etale sheafification, if $k$ is not algebraically closed) is representable by a $k$-group scheme $\Pic_{C/k}$, locally of finite type. This is FGA's main representability theorem (Grothendieck, FGA~232); the smooth-proper-geometrically-connected hypothesis ensures that the cohomology-and-base-change machinery applies and that $H^0(C, \mathcal O_C) = k$, which is needed for the quotient by $\pi^*\Pic(T)$ to be \'etale-locally injective.
328	
329	    \item[\textbf{(A.3)}] \emph{The identity component.} Let $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ be the identity component of $\Pic_{C/k}$. As the identity component of a $k$-group scheme locally of finite type, $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is geometrically irreducible; for $C$ smooth proper of genus $g$, it is moreover a smooth proper $k$-group scheme of dimension $g$. Smoothness comes from the deformation-theoretic identification of its tangent space at the identity with $H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ and homogeneity of the group action; properness from the fact that the natural map $\Pic^0_{C/k} \to \Pic_{C/k}$ is an open and closed immersion onto a proper component (a consequence of the cohomological criterion: $\Pic^0_{C/k}$ is the kernel of the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$, which is locally constant). Take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$.
330	
331	    \item[\textbf{(A.4)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi universal morphism.} For each $P \in C(k)$, the Abel--Jacobi morphism
332	    \[
333	      \iota_P \colon C \to \Pic^0_{C/k}, \qquad
334	      Q \longmapsto [\mathcal O_C(Q - P)]
335	    \]
336	    sends $P$ to the identity of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$. It is universal among morphisms $f \colon C \to A$ to smooth proper geometrically irreducible $k$-group schemes $A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$: this is the classical Albanese property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, proved by reducing to the case $k = \overline k$ (Galois descent), where it is a standard consequence of the autoduality of the Jacobian and the fact that line bundles on $C \times A$ trivialised along $\{P\} \times A$ correspond bijectively to morphisms $C \to A^{\vee} = A$ sending $P$ to $\eta_A$.
337	  \end{itemize}
338	
339	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route A.} Each sub-step is missing.
340	  \begin{itemize}
341	    \item Sub-step A.1 requires a working theory of relative line bundles on a product $C \times_k T$ and the descent of the abelian-group structure on $\Pic$ along $\pi^*$. The pieces are present individually but not assembled.
342	    \item Sub-step A.2 requires FGA representability for $\Pic_{C/k}$, which in turn requires Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure not in Mathlib (\texttt{HilbertScheme}, \texttt{QuotScheme}, and the cohomology-and-base-change for proper morphisms with finitely presented kernels). The construction engine for this Quot/Hilbert layer is Nitsure, ``Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes'' (FGA Explained; arXiv:math/0504590), \S\,4 (generic flatness and the flattening stratification) and \S\,5 (the construction of $\mathrm{Quot}$, hence $\mathrm{Hilb}$, as a scheme); see \texttt{references/nitsure-hilbert-quot.md}.
343	    \item Sub-step A.3 requires connectedness machinery for group schemes locally of finite type, the identity-component construction $G^0 \subseteq G$, and the degree map $\Pic_{C/k} \to \underline{\mathbb Z}_k$.
344	    \item Sub-step A.4 requires the universal property of $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, which is the classical Albanese functoriality and is not in Mathlib in this form.
345	  \end{itemize}
346	
347	  \paragraph{Route B --- Symmetric powers and Stein factorisation (historical alternative; not pursued).}
348	  \textbf{Iter-144 disposition.} Route B is a historical alternative \emph{not pursued} by the project. It is preserved here for scholarly context only. Route A --- Picard scheme via FGA --- is the committed M3 route per STRATEGY.md \S~M3 iter-144 disposition. The decomposition, gating Mathlib pieces, and midpoint LOC figure ($\sim 9000$ LOC) recorded below are the iter-123 audit values (\texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md}); the chapter does not re-cost Route B under current Mathlib snapshots.
349	
350	  This route follows Milne's \emph{Abelian Varieties} (Chapter~III) and avoids FGA representability at the cost of requiring quotients of schemes by finite group actions.
351	
352	  \begin{itemize}
353	    \item[\textbf{(B.1)}] \emph{The $g$-fold symmetric power.} Form the product $C^g$ and let $S_g$ act by permutation of the factors. The categorical quotient $C^{(g)} := C^g / S_g$ exists as a $k$-scheme, smooth and proper over $k$ of dimension $g$. Smoothness follows because the action of $S_g$ on the smooth $k$-scheme $C^g$ is free outside the diagonal locus and the local-ring computation at the diagonal points produces a smooth ring (this is the symmetric-product computation of Fogarty / Mumford). Properness descends from properness of $C^g$ via the geometric-quotient property.
354	
355	    \item[\textbf{(B.2)}] \emph{The Abel--Jacobi morphism on symmetric powers.} Fix a $k$-rational point $P_0 \in C(k)$. The map
356	    \[
357	      \sigma \colon C^{(g)} \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}, \qquad
358	      \sum_{i=1}^g [Q_i] \longmapsto \mathcal O_C\bigl(\textstyle\sum_{i=1}^g Q_i\bigr)

1	\chapter{Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form; Mumford \S4 in the abelian-variety case)}
2	\label{chap:Rigidity}
3	
4	This chapter records a self-contained helper: \emph{rigidity for morphisms of schemes that agree on a non-empty open}. The classical motivation is Mumford's rigidity for abelian-variety morphisms, but the project's formalised statement is more general: the source-side group-object hypothesis is unused by the proof and is dropped.
5	
6	The lemma is independent of the construction of $\Jac(C)$. It is the load-bearing input for the M2.a sub-step of the genus-0 Albanese-witness argument (Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b), where the source is taken to be $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ (which is \emph{not} a group scheme).
7	
8	\section{Statement}
9	
10	\begin{theorem}\leanok
11	[Rigidity for morphisms of schemes (scheme-level form)]
12	  \label{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen}
13	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen}
14	  Let $k$ be a field. Let $X$ be a reduced geometrically irreducible $k$-scheme and let $Y$ be a separated $k$-scheme. Let $g_1, g_2 \colon X \to Y$ be two morphisms of $k$-schemes, and let $U \subseteq X$ be a non-empty open subset. If
15	  \[
16	    U.\iota \circ g_1 \;=\; U.\iota \circ g_2
17	  \]
18	  as scheme morphisms (where $U.\iota \colon U \hookrightarrow X$ is the canonical open immersion), then $g_1 = g_2$ as morphisms of $k$-schemes.
19	\end{theorem}
20	
21	\begin{remark}
22	  The classical Mumford rigidity theorem (\emph{Abelian Varieties}, \S4) usually states the special case where $Y$ is an abelian variety and the conclusion is phrased as ``a morphism vanishing on a non-empty open is zero''. The two forms are interderivable in the abelian-variety case via the difference morphism $\delta = g_1 \cdot g_2^{-1}$ formed from the group law on $Y$. The project formalises the more general statement above because the M2.a (genus-0 Albanese-witness) consumer specifically needs the source to be $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, which is not a group scheme: the abelian-variety packaging is consumer-side and the scheme-level statement is what the proof actually establishes.
23	
24	  The refactor that landed the scheme-level form (iter-125; see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md}) dropped 8 unused hypotheses from the project's original group-scheme-flavoured declaration and weakened the target-side $[\mathtt{IsProper}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$ to $[\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$.
25	\end{remark}
26	
27	\section{Proof sketch}
28	
29	\begin{proof}
30	  \leanok
31	  We reduce to Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}), which gives the standard scheme-level fact that two morphisms from a reduced scheme to a separated scheme agreeing on a dominant subscheme agree everywhere.
32	
33	  The three load-bearing instances feeding that closure are:
34	  \begin{itemize}
35	    \item \emph{$Y \to \Spec k$ is separated.} Hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ Y.\mathrm{hom}]$ provides this directly (no projection from $\mathtt{IsProper}$ needed). The \texttt{OverClass.fromOver} instance transports it to the form $\mathtt{IsSeparated}\ (Y.\mathrm{left} \downarrow \Spec k)$ that the closure step expects.
36	    \item \emph{$X$ is an irreducible space.} Hypothesis $[\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}\ X.\mathrm{hom}]$ over the field-spectrum base $\Spec k$ (which is a single point, hence subsingleton) yields $\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\ X.\mathrm{left}$ via \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
37	    \item \emph{The inclusion $U.\iota$ is dominant.} A non-empty open in an irreducible space is dense, and a dense open immersion is dominant: \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι}.
38	  \end{itemize}
39	  With these three derived instances and the hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsReduced}\ X.\mathrm{left}]$, the closure \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} produces the underlying scheme-morphism equality $g_1.\mathrm{left} = g_2.\mathrm{left}$ from the agreement-on-$U$ hypothesis. The promotion to $g_1 = g_2$ in $\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)$ uses \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext}.
40	
41	  \medskip
42	  \noindent\textbf{Mathlib ingredients.}
43	  \begin{itemize}
44	    \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} for the scheme-level closure step.
45	    \item \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton} to derive irreducibility of $X.\mathrm{left}$.
46	    \item \texttt{Scheme.PartialMap.Opens.isDominant\_ι} to derive dominance of the open immersion $U.\iota$ from the non-empty hypothesis.
47	    \item \texttt{Over.OverMorphism.ext} to promote underlying scheme equality to $\mathrm{Over}$-morphism equality.
48	    \item No abelian-variety / group-object infrastructure is needed: smoothness, properness, and group-object hypotheses on $X$ and $Y$ are all dropped from the formalised statement.
49	  \end{itemize}
50	\end{proof}
51	
52	\section{Use in the project}
53	
54	The rigidity theorem is used in two places downstream:
55	
56	\begin{itemize}
57	  \item \textbf{M2.a (genus-0 Albanese witness).} Jacobian.tex \S C.2.b reduces the genus-0 rigidity-over-$\bar k$ argument (for $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ hitting the identity at a $\bar k$-point) to this theorem with $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ and $Y := A_{\bar k}$. The refactor of iter-125 (dropping the source-side group-object hypothesis) is what enables this application. The named project declaration packaging the C.2.a–C.2.e reduction is \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} in \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}; that chapter scaffolds the iter-126 Lean target \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity\_over\_kbar} with the C.2.d cotangent-vanishing keystone residual.
58	  \item \textbf{Uniqueness in the Albanese property.} Used in the uniqueness half of Theorem~\ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} (the Albanese property of $\Jac(C)$): given two morphisms $g_1, g_2 \colon \Jac(C) \to A$ with $g_1 \circ \alpha_P = g_2 \circ \alpha_P$, scheme-level agreement on the image of $\alpha_P$ (a non-empty open after passing to $C^{(g)} \twoheadrightarrow \Jac(C)$) forces $g_1 = g_2$.
59	\end{itemize}
60	
61	Note: the existence half of \ref{thm:exists_unique_ofCurve_comp} requires Phase~B/C infrastructure (Picard scheme, dual abelian variety). Rigidity alone yields \emph{uniqueness} only.
62	
63	\section{Mathlib status}
64	
65	The scheme-level form is a thin specialization of Mathlib's existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated}); the project's contribution is the $\mathrm{Over}\ (\Spec k)$-bundled packaging plus the convenience derivations of \texttt{IrreducibleSpace} and \texttt{IsDominant} from the project-side hypotheses.
66	
67	An upstream-PR candidate is a thin corollary in Mathlib's \texttt{Separated} file:
68	\[
69	  \texttt{ext\_of\_eqOnNonemptyOpen} \;\colon\; \texttt{(non-empty open) + (reduced source) + (separated target) }\Rightarrow\texttt{ ext}
70	\]
71	packaging the dense-and-dominant chain into a single API call. The incremental value over the existing \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} is small (just the API convenience of "non-empty open" over "dominant morphism"); see \texttt{analogies/rigidity-refactor.md} for the proposed statement and home file. The project does not pursue this upstream PR as part of the iter-125 work.
72

359	    \]
360	    is well-defined (the right-hand side is symmetric in the $Q_i$ and depends only on the unordered tuple) and is surjective. By Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, $\sigma$ is birational onto its image: a generic divisor of degree $g$ on $C$ has $h^0(\mathcal O_C(D)) = 1$, so the fibre $\sigma^{-1}([\mathcal O_C(D)])$ is a point at generic divisors; the special fibres are projective spaces $\mathbb P(h^0 - 1)$ over the locus where $h^0 > 1$.
361	
362	    \item[\textbf{(B.3)}] \emph{The Stein factorisation.} Because $\sigma$ is a proper surjection with geometrically connected fibres (each fibre $\mathbb P^{h^0-1}_k$ is geometrically irreducible), its Stein factorisation
363	    \[
364	      C^{(g)} \xrightarrow{\;\sigma_*\;} \Spec_{\Pic^g_{C/k}}\bigl(\sigma_* \mathcal O_{C^{(g)}}\bigr) \longrightarrow \Pic^g_{C/k}
365	    \]
366	    presents the second arrow as a finite morphism whose source is the intermediate scheme. Since the fibres of $\sigma$ are connected, this intermediate scheme equals $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ scheme-theoretically (the Stein factorisation is trivial on the second arrow when fibres are connected, by Stein's classical theorem). The middle scheme is therefore $\Pic^g_{C/k}$ itself, which is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of dimension $g$. Translating by $-g P_0$ yields $\Pic^0_{C/k}$, and we take $J := \Pic^0_{C/k}$ as in Route A. The universal property is then established as in Sub-step A.4.
367	  \end{itemize}
368	
369	  \emph{Mathlib status for Route B.} Each sub-step is blocked by a different missing piece.
370	  \begin{itemize}
371	    \item Sub-step B.1 requires symmetric powers of schemes (the construction $\Sym^g$ for a $k$-scheme of finite type) and finite-group-scheme quotients of schemes (the quotient $C^g / S_g$). Mathlib has finite-group quotients of types and of various algebraic structures, but not yet a usable scheme-level quotient by a finite group action with the smooth-target property.
372	    \item Sub-step B.2 requires Brill--Noether--Riemann--Roch, including the relative version for families of curves --- the cohomology-and-base-change machinery for proper morphisms.
373	    \item Sub-step B.3 requires the Stein factorisation theorem for proper morphisms of locally Noetherian schemes ($f \colon X \to Y$ proper $\implies$ $f_* \mathcal O_X$ is a coherent $\mathcal O_Y$-algebra). Mathlib has partial coherence-theoretic infrastructure but does not yet have the cohomology of $\mathcal O$-modules of finite type along proper morphisms in the form needed.
374	  \end{itemize}
375	
376	  \paragraph{Genus-$0$ sub-case (absorbed into the same witness).}
377	  When $g(C) = 0$ the witness's underlying scheme is $J = \Spec k$, and the universal property reduces to a rigidity statement.
378	
379	  \begin{itemize}
380	    \item[\textbf{(C.1)}] \emph{Brauer--Severi identification.} A smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve $C$ over $k$ with $g(C) = 0$ and a $k$-rational point $P \in C(k)$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb P^1_k$. This is the Brauer--Severi triviality criterion: a Brauer--Severi variety of dimension $1$ is $\mathbb P^1$ over its base if and only if it has a rational point. The genus-$0$ identification on the curve side uses $h^0(C, \mathcal O_C(P)) = 2$ (Riemann--Roch) to produce a degree-$1$ morphism $C \to \mathbb P^1_k$, which is then an isomorphism by degree-and-dimension.
381	
382	    \item[\textbf{(C.2)}] \emph{Rigidity for morphisms from $\mathbb P^1$ to an abelian variety.} We state the rigidity result over the algebraic closure $\bar k$, since the protected signature of Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} does not assume $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ and the genus-$0$ identification $C \cong \mathbb P^1$ is only available after passing to $\bar k$ (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexamples to a $k$-side identification without a rational point). The conclusion over $k$ --- equality of the given morphism with the constant morphism as $k$-schemes --- then follows by \emph{descent}: a morphism equality that holds after base change to $\bar k$ descends to $k$ along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (Sub-step~C.2.f).
383	
384	    \begin{itemize}
385	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.a)}] \emph{Statement (over $\bar k$).} Let $A$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$, with identity element $\eta_A \in A(\bar k)$. Let
386	      \[
387	        f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \longrightarrow A
388	      \]
389	      be a morphism of $\bar k$-schemes such that $f(p) = \eta_A$ for some closed point $p \in \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}(\bar k)$. Then $f$ factors as the composite $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k \xrightarrow{\,\eta_A\,} A$; equivalently, $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$.
390	
391	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.b)}] \emph{Reduction to the project's rigidity lemma.} Let $c \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ denote the constant morphism at $\eta_A$ --- the unique $\bar k$-morphism factoring through the closed-point section $\eta_A \colon \Spec \bar k \hookrightarrow A$. The conclusion $f = c$ follows from Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} applied to $g_1 := f$, $g_2 := c$, $X := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$, $Y := A$, once we produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $U.\iota \circ f = U.\iota \circ c$ as scheme morphisms. (After the iter-125 refactor, the cited theorem is stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated; the source-side group-object decoration that the earlier formulation carried has been dropped. $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ satisfies the refactored hypotheses trivially --- it is smooth, hence reduced; it is geometrically irreducible over $\bar k$; and $A$ is proper hence separated over $\bar k$.) The reduction therefore amounts to: produce a non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ on which $f$ and $c$ agree as scheme morphisms; we will in fact take $U = \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$.
392	
393	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.c)}] \emph{Image dimension argument.} The morphism $f$ goes from a proper geometrically integral $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $1$ to a smooth proper $\bar k$-scheme of dimension $g := \dim_{\bar k} A$. The set-theoretic image $f(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) \subseteq A$ is an irreducible closed subset of $A$ (the continuous image of an irreducible space under a proper map is irreducible and closed) of dimension at most $1$. If $g = 0$ then $A = \Spec \bar k$ and the conclusion is vacuous: the unique $\bar k$-morphism to $\Spec \bar k$ is the constant morphism. Henceforth assume $g \geq 1$, in which case the image is either a single point (the desired conclusion) or a one-dimensional irreducible proper closed subscheme of $A$ --- an irreducible rational curve in $A$.
394	
395	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.d)}] \emph{Key classical input: proper rational curves on an abelian variety are constant.} The remaining case --- image of dimension exactly $1$ --- is ruled out by the following classical fact (Mumford, \emph{Abelian Varieties}, Chapter~II~\S4, Proposition):
396	
397	      \begin{quote}
398	        \emph{Let $A$ be an abelian variety of positive dimension over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$. Then every morphism $f \colon \mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is constant.}
399	      \end{quote}
400	
401	      Two standard proofs are available; both unwind a few layers of upstream Mathlib infrastructure but are routine once the input lemmas are present.
402	      \begin{itemize}
403	        \item \emph{Via the dual abelian variety.} Pullback of line bundles along $f$ induces a homomorphism of group schemes $f^{\vee} \colon A^{\vee} \to \Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k})$, where $A^{\vee}$ is the dual abelian variety (the moduli space of degree-zero line bundles on $A$). The Picard group of $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ has trivial degree-zero component, $\Pic^0(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}) = 0$, because every line bundle on $\mathbb P^1$ of degree $0$ is isomorphic to $\mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}$ (Hartshorne, \emph{Algebraic Geometry}, II.6 and II.7). Hence $f^{\vee} = 0$. By autoduality of abelian varieties ($A^{\vee\vee} = A$ canonically, double duality of the Poincar\'e line bundle), the zero of $f^{\vee}$ implies that $f$ itself is constant.
404	
405	        \item \emph{Via the cotangent bundle.} The cotangent sheaf $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ of an abelian variety is a trivial $\mathcal O_A$-module: the tangent bundle of a group scheme over a field is generated globally by translates of the Lie algebra at the identity, hence is trivial, hence so is its dual. Pulling back along $f$ yields a morphism of locally free sheaves $f^{*} \Omega_{A/\bar k} \to \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}$, whose global sections form a $\bar k$-linear map
406	        \[
407	          H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, f^{*}\Omega_{A/\bar k}\bigr) \longrightarrow H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \Omega_{\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}/\bar k}\bigr) = H^0\bigl(\mathbb P^1_{\bar k},\, \mathcal O_{\mathbb P^1}(-2)\bigr) = 0.
408	        \]
409	        Triviality of $\Omega_{A/\bar k}$ means the left-hand side is a non-zero $\bar k$-vector space (of dimension $g$); the only way the map can land in zero is for it to be the zero map at every fibre, i.e.\ for the differential $df$ to vanish identically on $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$. Smoothness of $A$ over $\bar k$ together with the vanishing of $df$ forces $f$ to be a constant morphism on the geometric points and hence (by reduced-source / separated-target, see C.2.e) as a scheme morphism.
410	      \end{itemize}
411	      Either proof yields: $f$ is constant. The pointed condition $f(p) = \eta_A$ then identifies the constant value, so $f = c$ on underlying topological points.
412	
413	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.e)}] \emph{Promoting set-level equality to scheme equality.} The set-level equality $f = c$ on underlying topological points (established in C.2.d) promotes to scheme-morphism equality on any non-empty open $U \subseteq \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ via the standard reduced-source / separated-target argument: $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ is reduced (smooth implies regular, regular implies reduced) and $A$ is separated over $\bar k$ (proper implies separated), so a $\bar k$-morphism $\mathbb P^1_{\bar k} \to A$ is determined by its values on the underlying topological space. In particular, $f|_U = c|_U$ as scheme morphisms for the open $U := \mathbb P^1_{\bar k}$ itself, and the reduction of C.2.b gives $f = c$ as scheme morphisms.
414	
415	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.f)}] \emph{Descent of morphism equality from $\bar k$ to $k$.} The rigidity conclusion of Sub-step~C.2.d is proved over the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$, where \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} now carries the hypothesis $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$. To obtain the corresponding equality of morphisms over the base field $k$, we descend along the base change $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$.
416	
417	      Concretely, let $f, c \colon C \to A$ be two $k$-morphisms (in the application $f$ is the given map and $c$ the constant map at $\eta_A$). Base-change along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ produces $f_{\bar k}, c_{\bar k} \colon C_{\bar k} \to A_{\bar k}$ together with the projections $p \colon C_{\bar k} = C \times_k \bar k \to C$ and $q \colon A_{\bar k} \to A$, fitting the base-change squares $q \circ f_{\bar k} = f \circ p$ and $q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p$. Suppose $f_{\bar k} = c_{\bar k}$ (this is what \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} delivers over $\bar k$). Then $f \circ p = q \circ f_{\bar k} = q \circ c_{\bar k} = c \circ p$, so $f$ and $c$ agree after pre-composition with $p$.
418	
419	      It remains to right-cancel $p$. The morphism $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ is faithfully flat (a field extension is a faithfully flat ring map) and surjective; both properties are stable under base change, so the projection $p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C$ is faithfully flat and surjective. By \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}, $p$ is therefore an epimorphism of schemes, and right-cancelling it in $f \circ p = c \circ p$ gives $f = c$ over $k$. This is the entire descent: a faithfully-flat surjective cover induces an epimorphism, hence the base-change map is injective on the $\mathrm{Hom}$-set into $A$. The step is a $\sim 2$-line consequence of the named Mathlib lemma plus base-change-square commutativity, and is cheaper than the proper-cohomology flat-base-change machinery a cohomological descent would have required.
420	
421	      \item[\textbf{(C.2.g)}] \emph{Mathlib gap statement.} The keystone fact of Sub-step~C.2.d --- that every morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ to a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over $\bar k$ is constant --- is \emph{not} present in current Mathlib (snapshot \texttt{b80f227}) in any form usable here. The project records it as the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, whose base field now carries $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ (the Lean identifier retains the \texttt{kbar} name, which is now accurate). The keystone decomposes into the shared cotangent-vanishing pile of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)--(iii): (i) cotangent triviality of a group scheme over $\bar k$ via the functorial shear iso; (ii) scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec \bar k$ via the \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass plus the iter-125-refactored \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}; (iii) characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$. Each is an upstream Mathlib gap-fill in its own right (see \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}). When $g = \dim A = 0$ the statement is vacuous and the declaration's conclusion is automatic. The reduction step C.2.b reuses the project's already-formalised Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (after the iter-125 refactor, this is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, stated at the scheme level: source reduced and geometrically irreducible, target separated); the promotion step C.2.e uses standard reduced-source / separated-target lemmas, partially in Mathlib. The descent step C.2.f --- from $\bar k$ back to the base field $k$ --- is \emph{not} a Mathlib gap: it is a short ($\sim 2$-line) consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} (already in Mathlib, in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Flat}) together with base-change-square commutativity, and is cheaper than the proper-cohomology flat-base-change machinery a cohomological descent would have required.
422	    \end{itemize}
423	
424	    \item[\textbf{(C.3)}] \emph{The trivial witness.} Take $J := \Spec k$, the terminal $k$-scheme. The group-object structure on $\Spec k$ is trivial; it is smooth proper geometrically irreducible of relative dimension $0 = g(C)$. The unique morphism $\iota_P \colon C \to \Spec k$ sends $P$ to the (unique) point. By Sub-step C.2 every $f \colon C \to A$ with $f(P) = \eta_A$ is constant at $\eta_A$, hence factors uniquely through the unique morphism $g \colon \Spec k \to A$ sending the point to $\eta_A$; uniqueness of $g$ follows because $\Spec k \to A$ is determined by its value on the unique $k$-rational point of $\Spec k$.
425	  \end{itemize}
426	
427	  \emph{Mathlib status for the genus-$0$ sub-case.}
428	  \begin{itemize}
429	    \item Sub-step C.1 requires the genus-$0$ identification on the curve side, which in turn requires Serre duality / the basic Riemann--Roch infrastructure that this project has explicitly trimmed (see the project's Phase~C exit policy). Mathlib has partial $\mathbb P^1$-theory but the curve-side genus-$0$ identification is missing.
430	    \item Sub-step C.2 reduces, via the project's already-formalised rigidity lemma (Theorem~\ref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} of Chapter~\ref{chap:Rigidity}), to the classical fact that proper rational curves on a positive-dimensional smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$ are constant (the keystone of Sub-step~C.2.d, packaged as \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}). This classical fact is not in Mathlib in the form needed here; see Sub-step~C.2.g for the explicit gap statement and the inventory of pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile. The descent of morphism equality from $\bar k$ back to the base field $k$ (Sub-step~C.2.f) is \emph{not} a Mathlib gap: it is a short consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective} together with base-change-square commutativity (see above).
431	    \item Sub-step C.3 is unconditional once C.1 and C.2 are in place.
432	  \end{itemize}
433	
434	  \paragraph{Mathlib infrastructure summary.}
435	  Per the iter-144 M3 disposition (STRATEGY.md \S~M3), the project commits to Route A for the positive-genus arm and treats Route B as a historical alternative not pursued. The Mathlib build-outs gating the two surviving arms are therefore:
436	  \begin{itemize}
437	    \item[\textbf{($\alpha$)}] \emph{Hilbert / Quot scheme infrastructure plus FGA representability (COMMITTED M3 infrastructure --- iter-144 disposition).} This is the committed Mathlib build-out that unlocks Route A, which in turn discharges the positive-genus arm of the witness existence (\cref{def:positiveGenusWitness}). The single highest-cost build-out, since Hilbert/Quot schemes are upstream of much of modern algebraic geometry; but once present, the rest of Route A (identity component, smoothness, properness, universal property) follows by classical arguments already widely formalised in adjacent settings.
438	    \item[\textbf{($\gamma$)}] \emph{The rigidity theorem for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme, over an algebraically closed field $\bar k$.} This is the prerequisite of the genus-$0$ sub-case under Route A. The project packages it as \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (\cref{chap:RigidityKbar}), whose base field now carries $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$, gated on the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of \cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}: cotangent triviality of a group scheme over $\bar k$ via the functorial shear iso; scheme-level $\mathrm d f = 0 \Rightarrow$ $f$ factors through $\Spec \bar k$ via the \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass; characteristic-$p$ handling via the intrinsic absolute Frobenius $F_X$. The genus-$0$ arm of the witness existence is packaged as \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}), which over a general base field $k$ \emph{descends} the over-$\bar k$ rigidity conclusion to $k$: for each $k$-rational marked point $P \in C(k)$ it base-changes the data $(C, A, f, P)$ along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve, so $C_{\bar k}$ is again a genus-$0$ curve), applies \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} over $\bar k$ to conclude $f_{\bar k}$ is constant, then descends the morphism equality back to $k$ along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (Sub-step~C.2.f, via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}). The $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch is vacuously true (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$). Independently of ($\alpha$), establishing ($\gamma$) together with the short descent step lets the genus-$0$ part of the witness be discharged unconditionally via \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}; the higher-genus part is packaged as \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness}, iter-134 scaffold) whose body closure is gated on Route A ($\alpha$). Per the iter-135 body restructure of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, the witness existence is now a case-split $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} respectively, converting the formerly inline \texttt{sorry} into honest delegation to the two named scaffolds.
439	    \item[\textbf{($\beta$, historical only --- not pursued).}] \emph{Symmetric powers of schemes plus finite-group-scheme quotients plus Stein factorisation for proper morphisms.} Documented as a historical alternative not pursued by the project (Route B above); see \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route B for context. This entry is retained for scholarly continuity only; under the iter-144 disposition, ($\beta$) is \emph{not} a build-out the project is gating any of its deliverables on, and ($\beta$) does not appear on any active critical path.
440	  \end{itemize}
441	
442	  In sum: Mathlib currently contains neither of the two active infrastructure builds (($\alpha$) and ($\gamma$)), and the existence statement is recorded here as the project's single explicit foundational hypothesis. It backs all downstream consequences --- the four protected instances on $\Jac(C)$ (Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred}), and the Abel--Jacobi map and its universal property of Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} --- and is discharged once ($\alpha$) and ($\gamma$) both land. The historical entry ($\beta$) is preserved for scholarly context only and is not part of the project's discharge path.
443	
444	  \paragraph{Iter-135 body restructure.} The Lean body of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} is, per iter-135 refactor, a case-split $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ delegating to \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} (genus-$0$ arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing iter-151+ under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot --- see \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} body closure status, revised iter-145) and \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} (positive-genus arm, \texttt{sorry} body closing M3 --- M3 is committed to Route A per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md \S~M3; sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window). The restructure converts the inline \texttt{sorry} on the witness-existence theorem itself into honest delegation to two named scaffolds; the witness existence is therefore conditionally established whenever both arms' bodies close (no Mathlib gap directly on the witness-existence theorem itself, only on each arm).
445	\end{proof}
446	
447	\subsection{The genus-$0$ arm of the witness existence}
448	\label{sec:genusZeroWitness}
449	
450	The genus-$0$ sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} admits an explicit witness construction at the Lean level, packaged separately from the general existence statement so that the genus-$0$ branch can be discharged independently once the shared cotangent-vanishing pile lands (\cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile} pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii)).
451	
452	\begin{theorem}\leanok
453	[Genus-$0$ Albanese witness]
454	  \label{def:genusZeroWitness}
455	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness}
456	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, thm:rigidity_over_kbar, def:genus}
457	  Let $C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with $\genus(C) = 0$. Then there exists a $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C$ whose underlying scheme is $\Spec k$ (the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$), with the trivial group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension $0$, properness, and geometric irreducibility, and whose $\mathtt{isAlbaneseFor}$ field encodes the Albanese universal property for every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$.
458	\end{theorem}
459	
460	\begin{proof}
461	  \uses{thm:rigidity_over_kbar, thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen, def:IsAlbanese, def:JacobianWitness, def:genus}
462	  We construct the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness}.
463	
464	  \paragraph{Underlying scheme.} Take $J := \mathbf{1}$, the terminal object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, which on underlying schemes is $\Spec k$ viewed as a $k$-scheme via the identity structure morphism.
465	
466	  \paragraph{Group-object structure, properness, smoothness, geometric irreducibility.} The terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ of any cartesian-monoidal category carries the (unique) trivial group-object structure: multiplication and inverse are the unique morphisms to $\mathbf{1}$, and the identity element is the identity morphism. The structure morphism $\mathbf{1}.\mathtt{hom} = \mathrm{id}_{\Spec k}$ is proper (the identity is a closed immersion, in particular proper), smooth (the identity is unramified and flat, hence smooth), and geometrically irreducible. The geometric-irreducibility instance is the project-local helper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.geometricallyIrreducible\_id\_Spec} (\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean:120}--\texttt{126}), which derives geometric irreducibility of the identity on $\Spec k$ from the fact that, on the geometric fibre $\Spec K$ over any field extension $k \to K$, the second projection of the pullback square is an isomorphism, and $\Spec K$ is irreducible.
467	
468	  \paragraph{Smoothness of relative dimension $\genus\,C$.} The smoothness witness $\mathtt{smoothGenus}$ is required at relative dimension $\genus\,C$. The hypothesis $\genus\,C = 0$ rewrites the target relative dimension to $0$, after which the goal reduces to $\mathtt{SmoothOfRelativeDimension}\ 0\ (\mathrm{id}_{\Spec k})$. This is a Mathlib instance: the identity morphism on any base is smooth of relative dimension $0$.
469	
470	  \paragraph{The \texttt{isAlbaneseFor} field.} For every $k$-rational marked point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$, we exhibit an \cref{def:IsAlbanese} term for the triple $(C, P, J)$.
471	
472	  The universal pointed morphism is the unique terminal map $\alpha := \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \colon C \to \mathbf{1}$ (the structure morphism of $C$ as a $k$-scheme, factored through the terminal object). The pointed condition $P \circ \alpha = \eta_J = \mathrm{id}_{\mathbf{1}}$ holds automatically: both sides are morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to \mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$, and the terminal object has a unique endomorphism.
473	
474	  For the universal factorisation: given a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme $A$ over $k$ with identity element $\eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$ and a morphism $f \colon C \to A$ with $P \circ f = \eta_A$, we must exhibit a unique morphism $g \colon J \to A$ such that $f = \alpha \circ g$. \emph{Existence}: take $g := \eta_A \colon \mathbf{1} \to A$. Then $\alpha \circ g = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$, which equals $f$ precisely when $f$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. We obtain this equality by descent from the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$. Base-change the data along $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$: the curve $C_{\bar k}$ is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible of genus $0$ (genus is stable under base change for a geometrically irreducible curve), the group scheme $A_{\bar k}$ is again smooth proper geometrically irreducible, the base-changed point $P_{\bar k}$ is a $\bar k$-rational point, and $f_{\bar k}$ still kills it: $f_{\bar k}(P_{\bar k}) = \eta_{A_{\bar k}}$. By \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (the project's named rigidity declaration of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, now carrying $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$), $f_{\bar k}$ equals the constant morphism $\mathtt{toUnit}\,C_{\bar k} \circ \eta_{A_{\bar k}}$ over $\bar k$. Finally we descend this morphism equality back to $k$: the projection $p \colon C_{\bar k} \to C$ is the base change of the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$, hence faithfully flat and surjective, hence an epimorphism of schemes by \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}; right-cancelling $p$ against the base-change-square identities yields $f = \mathtt{toUnit}\,C \circ \eta_A$ as $k$-scheme morphisms (Sub-step~C.2.f of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}). \emph{Uniqueness}: any two morphisms $\mathbf{1} \to A$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ agreeing on $P \circ (-) = \eta_A$ are equal by the universal property of the terminal object $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ as a category (a morphism out of $\mathbf{1}$ in $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ is determined by where it sends the unique generator, here pinned to $\eta_A$).
475	
476	  % NOTE: (iter-155 review) Two prose-accuracy items vs the iter-155 Lean skeleton
477	  % (`AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness`), both for a blueprint-writer to land:
478	  % (1) The "Uniqueness" sentence above justifies uniqueness of $g$ by "the
479	  % universal property of the terminal object $\mathbf 1$ ... a morphism out of
480	  % $\mathbf 1$ is determined by where it sends the unique generator". This is
481	  % mathematically loose: morphisms OUT of a terminal object are global points and
482	  % are NOT unique in general (the terminal property gives uniqueness of morphisms
483	  % INTO $\mathbf 1$). The formalized Lean proof instead uses the sound
484	  % epi-cancellation argument: cancel $\mathtt{toUnit}\,C$, whose underlying
485	  % $C.\mathtt{hom}$ is faithfully flat + surjective hence an epimorphism
486	  % (`Over.toUnit_left` + `Flat.epi_of_flat_of_surjective` + `Over.epi_of_epi_left`
487	  % + `cancel_epi`). Rewrite the uniqueness paragraph to the epi-cancellation form.
488	  % (2) The "now carrying $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$" description of
489	  % `rigidity_over_kbar` (here and in C.2.g / infrastructure-(γ) / Layer I) understates
490	  % its real signature: `RigidityKbar.lean:75-76` carries BOTH
491	  % $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]\ [\mathtt{CharZero}\ \bar k]$ — char-$p$ is
492	  % excluded by hypothesis, NOT yet Frobenius-handled. The genus-0 Lean witness
493	  % correctly flags this gap (its own char-$p$ arm is unbacked). Note the current
494	  % `[CharZero kbar]` restriction in the prose; piece (iii)/Frobenius is aspirational.
495	  \paragraph{Vacuity for the $C(k) = \emptyset$ branch.} When no $k$-rational point $P : \mathbf{1} \to C$ exists (Brauer--Severi conics over $\mathbb Q$ are the standard counterexample), the universal quantifier $\forall (P : \mathbf{1} \to C), \mathtt{IsAlbanese}\,C\,P\,J$ is vacuously satisfied at the Lean type level: Lean's $\forall$ over an empty type is trivially true. The construction handles this branch implicitly without any explicit case split.
496	
497	  \paragraph{Body closure status.} The Lean body of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genusZeroWitness} (in \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Jacobian.lean}) is currently \texttt{sorry}; the closure is gated on the body of \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} (M2.a), which in turn depends on pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii) of the shared cotangent-vanishing pile (\cref{sec:RigidityKbar_shared_pile}). Under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot (and the iter-145 Wave-2 chart-algebra envelope), the revised trajectory is: iter-146+ chart-algebra piece (ii) sub-piece prover lane; iter-149+ M2.a body closure (\cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar}); iter-151+ M2.b body closure (\cref{def:genusZeroWitness}). Earliest realistic body-closure iteration: \textbf{iter-151+ (revised iter-145; under the iter-144 chart-algebra pivot)}.
498	\end{proof}
499	
500	\subsection{The positive-genus arm of the witness existence}
501	\label{sec:positiveGenusWitness}
502	
503	The positive-genus sub-case of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} is packaged separately from the genus-$0$ arm so that the two arms can be closed independently. Whereas the genus-$0$ arm \cref{def:genusZeroWitness} routes through the rigidity declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar}, the positive-genus arm requires the full Albanese construction via Route A (Picard scheme via FGA --- Hilbert/Quot infrastructure plus FGA representability) of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, which is the committed M3 route per the iter-144 disposition in STRATEGY.md \S~M3. Route B (symmetric powers + Stein factorisation) is a historical alternative not pursued by the project.
504	
505	\begin{theorem}\leanok
506	[Positive-genus Albanese witness]
507	  \label{def:positiveGenusWitness}
508	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness}
509	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, def:genus, def:IsAlbanese}
510	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman, The Picard scheme], Remark rmk:Jac (the Jacobian: $J:=\IPicz_{X/S}$ of a smooth projective family of genus-$g>0$ curves exists and is a projective Abelian $S$-scheme); arXiv:math/0504020, p.~50  (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex)
511	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Assume $X/S$ is projective and smooth, its geometric
512	  % fibers are connected curves of genus $g>0$, and $S$ is Noetherian.  Set
513	  % $J:=\IPicz_{X/S}$; it exists and is a projective Abelian $S$-scheme by
514	  % Exercise~\ref{ex:jac}.  Set $\wh J:=\IPicz_{J/S}$; it exists, is a
515	  % projective Abelian scheme, and is ``dual'' to $J$ by
516	  % Remark~\ref{rmk:Ablsch}."
517	  \textit{Source: Kleiman, The Picard Scheme, Remark (the Jacobian, rmk:Jac).}
518	  Let $C \in \mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec k)$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ with $\genus(C) \ge 1$. Then there exists a $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}\,C$ whose underlying scheme is the Albanese variety of $C$ (classically the identity component of the Picard scheme $\mathrm{Pic}^0_{C/k}$, or equivalently the Stein factorisation of the Abel--Jacobi morphism on $\mathrm{Sym}^g(C)$). The full $\mathtt{JacobianWitness}$ data (group-object structure, smoothness of relative dimension $g$, properness, geometric irreducibility, and Albanese universal property uniformly over $k$-rational marked points) is supplied by the Route A construction (per \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route A; Route B is documented as a historical alternative not pursued).
519	\end{theorem}
520	
521	\begin{proof}
522	  \uses{def:JacobianWitness, def:genus, def:IsAlbanese, thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}
523	  This is the M3 arm of the witness existence. M3 is \textbf{committed to Route A --- Picard scheme via FGA} (per STRATEGY.md \S~M3 iter-144 disposition; $\sim 6500$ LOC midpoint per the iter-123 audit \texttt{analogies/m3-route-audit.md}). The autonomous loop writes the $\sim 6500$-LOC Mathlib material directly in-tree; upstream PR extraction is an OPTIONAL downstream lift, not a project deliverable. The committed route produces the seven fields of \cref{def:JacobianWitness} in positive genus via the following Mathlib-prerequisite path:
524	  \begin{itemize}
525	    \item \emph{Route A: Hilbert/Quot infrastructure plus FGA representability of $\Pic_{C/k}$.} See \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route A for the four-step decomposition (A.1 relative Picard functor; A.2 representability; A.3 identity component; A.4 Abel--Jacobi universal morphism). The smallest entry point into Route A is the \texttt{RelativeSpec} functor (A.1 prerequisite; $\sim 700$--$1100$ LOC) per the iter-123 audit.
526	  \end{itemize}
527	  Route B (symmetric powers $C^{(g)} := C^g/S_g$ + Stein factorisation, $\sim 9000$ LOC midpoint per the iter-123 audit) is documented in \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} Route B as a historical alternative not pursued by the project.
528	
529	  M3 sits behind M2 critical-path during the M2 wait window; whether to start Route A scaffolding (smallest entry: \texttt{RelativeSpec} functor at $\sim 700$--$1100$ LOC) in parallel with M2 or strictly after M2 closes is a planner-level scheduling call. Existing iter-150 mathlib-analogist scheduling trigger: dispatched if cumulative M2.body-pile LOC exceeds $925$ LOC without piece (i.b) closing, OR if M2.a body closure has not landed by iter-160.
530	
531	  The scaffold \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness} (landed iter-134) is load-bearing iter-135 onward for the genus-stratified body restructure of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} (iter-135 refactor): the body case-splits $\mathtt{by\_cases}\ h : \genus\,C = 0$ and delegates to \cref{def:positiveGenusWitness} on the $\neg(\genus\,C = 0)$ arm via \texttt{Nat.pos\_of\_ne\_zero}. The Lean body of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.positiveGenusWitness} is currently \texttt{sorry}; the closure is contingent on Route A of \cref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness} being prosecuted in full.
532	\end{proof}
533	
534	\section{Implementation route via the Albanese functor}
535	
536	Because the full Picard-scheme representability theorem is not available in current Mathlib, the formal construction proceeds in two layers:
537	
538	\begin{enumerate}
539	  \item \textbf{Layer I --- direct definition.} The Jacobian of $C$ is defined uniformly as the underlying scheme of the Albanese witness provided by Theorem~\ref{thm:nonempty_jacobianWitness}, carrying the four properties of Theorems~\ref{thm:Jacobian_grpObj}--\ref{thm:Jacobian_geomIrred} (each obtained by projection from the witness). The genus-$0$ case is included: the witness's universal property in that case carries the rigidity content for morphisms from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible genus-$0$ curve to a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme. This rigidity is established over the algebraic closure $\bar k = \mathrm{AlgebraicClosure}\,k$ via the named declaration \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} of \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (now carrying $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$) and the shared cotangent-vanishing pile pieces (i)+(ii)+(iii); the conclusion over a general base field $k$ is then obtained by \emph{descending} the over-$\bar k$ morphism equality along the faithfully-flat surjection $\Spec \bar k \to \Spec k$ (a short consequence of \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Flat.epi\_of\_flat\_of\_surjective}). The genus-$0$ arm is packaged as the explicit Lean construction \cref{def:genusZeroWitness}: the underlying scheme is the terminal object $\Spec k$, the universal morphism in the $C(k) \neq \emptyset$ branch is the unique terminal map \texttt{toUnit}\,$C$, and the universal factorisation reduces to \cref{thm:rigidity_over_kbar} over $\bar k$ followed by descent to $k$.
540	
541	  \item \textbf{Layer II --- Abel--Jacobi compatibility.} Chapter~\ref{chap:AbelJacobi} introduces the Abel--Jacobi morphism $C \to \Jac(C)$ and shows that it is universal among morphisms from $C$ to abelian varieties.
542	\end{enumerate}

1	\chapter{Genus of a smooth proper curve}
2	\label{chap:Genus}
3	
4	Throughout this chapter, $k$ denotes a field and $C$ a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve over $k$ of relative dimension $1$. In other words, $C \to \Spec k$ is a morphism of schemes carrying the geometric typeclasses that make it a curve in the modern sense.
5	
6	\section{Definition}
7	
8	% NOTE: classical definition $g(C) = \dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$; see
9	% Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Ch.~IV \S1 (genus of a curve) — TOC
10	% summarised in `references/hartshorne-ag.md`. The finiteness backing
11	% (Serre on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme) is queued in
12	% `references/stacks-0BUG.md` and chapter
13	% \texttt{Cohomology\_StructureSheafModuleK}.
14	\begin{definition}\leanok
15	[Genus of a smooth proper curve]
16	  \label{def:genus}
17	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.genus}
18	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
19	  The \emph{genus} of a smooth proper curve $C$ over $k$ is the natural number
20	  \[
21	    g(C) \;=\; \dim_k H^1(C,\, \mathcal O_C),
22	  \]
23	  the $k$-dimension of the first sheaf-cohomology group of the structure sheaf of $C$.
24	\end{definition}
25	
26	\begin{proof}
27	  \uses{def:Scheme_HModule, def:Scheme_toModuleKSheaf}
28	  \leanok
29	  The definition is realised by passing the structure sheaf through the sheaf-of-$k$-modules construction and applying the $k$-linear sheaf cohomology $H^1$. The result is a $k$-vector space; its dimension is extracted via the standard finiteness rank.
30	
31	  \begin{remark}[Lean implementation]
32	    The formal definition uses the project's $\ModuleCat k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of the structure sheaf:
33	    \[
34	      \mathtt{genus}\,C \;:=\; \Module.\mathtt{finrank}\,k\,\bigl(\Scheme.\HModule\,k\,(\Scheme.\toModuleKSheaf\,C)\,1\bigr).
35	    \]
36	    The carrier $\toModuleKSheaf C$ is the sheaf-of-$k$-modules constructed from $\struct C$ via the structure morphism; the wrapper $\HModule k F 1$ is the $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology, given by $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$, which carries a canonical $\Module k$-structure inherited from the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category.
37	  \end{remark}
38	
39	  Mathlib's $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$ returns the $k$-dimension when the underlying module is finite-dimensional and zero otherwise. For a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve, Serre's theorem on coherent cohomology of a proper $k$-scheme guarantees $\dim_k H^1(C, \struct C) < \infty$, so $g(C)$ is the geometric genus on the relevant case. The closure is therefore the one-liner above, confirmed to typecheck against current Mathlib.
40	\end{proof}
41	
42	Equivalent reformulations available once Riemann--Roch and Serre duality are formalised:
43	\begin{itemize}
44	  \item $g(C) = \dim_k H^0(C,\, \Omega^1_{C/k})$ (Serre duality).
45	  \item $g(C) = 1 - \chi(\mathcal O_C)$, where $\chi$ is the Euler characteristic.
46	  \item For a smooth projective curve over $\mathbb C$, $g(C)$ equals the topological genus of $C(\mathbb C)$.
47	\end{itemize}
48	Each of these requires Mathlib infrastructure that is currently absent. The first reformulation in particular requires the sheaf of relative differentials of schemes (Phase~B), and the others require Riemann--Roch / Serre duality / a topological-vs-algebraic geometry comparison.
49	
50	\section{Mathlib gap}
51	\label{sec:Genus_status}
52	
53	The honest definition $\dim_k H^1(C, \mathcal O_C)$ requires a $\Module k$-flavoured sheaf cohomology of $\struct C$ on the topology of opens of the underlying space of $C$. Through earlier iterations the project has built this:
54	
55	\begin{itemize}
56	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\AddCommGrpCat$-valued sheaves} (Mathlib): defined as $\Ext$ from the constant sheaf at $\AddCommGrpCat.\mathrm{of}\,(\ULift\,\mathbb Z)$.
57	  \item \textbf{Sheaf cohomology of $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaves} (this project): the parallel $\Module k$-valued construction with unit object $\ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,k$ instead of $\ULift\,\mathbb Z$, gluing through the $\Linear k$-enrichment of the sheaf category to recover a $\Module k$ structure on the cohomology groups.
58	  \item \textbf{Structure sheaf as a $\ModuleCat k$-valued sheaf} (this project): for $C$ over $\Spec k$, the sheafification of the presheaf $U \mapsto \ModuleCat.\mathrm{of}\,k\,(\Gamma(C, U))$ where the $k$-action is the algebra structure induced by the structure morphism.
59	  \item \textbf{$H^0$ algebraic bridge} (this project): $H^0(C, \struct C)_{\Module k} \simeq_k \Hom_{\Sheaf}(\mathrm{const}, \toModuleKSheaf C)$, via Mathlib's $\Ext$ linear equivalence. Used downstream for the geometric content $H^0(C, \struct C) \simeq k$ on a connected proper $k$-curve.
60	\end{itemize}
61	
62	The remaining Mathlib gap for the \emph{theorem} (as opposed to the \emph{definition}) of genus:
63	
64	\begin{itemize}
65	  \item \textbf{Serre finiteness for $H^i(C, \mathcal F)$ on a proper $k$-curve.} Mathlib's $\ModuleCat.\mathtt{finite\_ext}$ provides $\Module.\mathtt{Finite}$ for module-Ext only, not sheaf-Ext. The Čech-cohomology scaffold is the recommended direction.
66	\end{itemize}
67	
68	\section{User authorisation of \texttt{noncomputable}}
69	\label{sec:Genus_noncomputable}
70	
71	The original challenge file writes $\mathtt{def\ genus}$, not $\mathtt{noncomputable\ def\ genus}$. Because $\Module.\mathtt{finrank}$, $\Abelian.\Ext.\mathtt{instModule}$, and the structure-sheaf-as-$k$-module construction are all noncomputable in Mathlib, the honest closure of the body requires the $\mathtt{noncomputable}$ modifier on the $\mathtt{def}$. The user explicitly authorised agents to add this modifier as a non-signature change: names, argument types, and argument order remain verbatim.
72	
73	\section{What this iteration does and does not}
74	
75	This iteration \emph{defines} the genus. It does not \emph{compute} it on any specific curve, and it does not prove that the result equals the geometric genus in the classical sense (which requires Serre finiteness for non-vacuity and Riemann--Roch for verification on examples). The downstream theorem $\mathtt{smoothOfRelativeDimension\_genus}$ in $\mathtt{Jacobian.lean}$ states that the Jacobian of $C$ is smooth of relative dimension $g(C)$ --- that theorem will require Serre finiteness on the way and is queued for the iteration that closes the Jacobian construction itself.
